html:
Chapter 27

Mr.¬ÝHaredale stood in the widow‚Äôs parlour with the door-key in his hand, gazing by turns at Mr.¬ÝChester and at Gabriel Varden, and occasionally glancing downward at the key as in the hope that of its own accord it would unlock the mystery; until Mr.¬ÝChester, putting on his hat and gloves, and sweetly inquiring whether they were walking in the same direction, recalled him to himself.

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúOur roads diverge‚ÅÝ‚Äîwidely, as you know. For the present, I shall remain here.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou will be hipped, Haredale; you will be miserable, melancholy, utterly wretched,‚Äù returned the other. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a place of the very last description for a man of your temper. I know it will make you very miserable.‚Äù

‚ÄúLet it,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝHaredale, sitting down; ‚Äúand thrive upon the thought. Good night!‚Äù

Feigning to be wholly unconscious of the abrupt wave of the hand which rendered this farewell tantamount to a dismissal, Mr.¬ÝChester retorted with a bland and heartfelt benediction, and inquired of Gabriel in what direction he was going.

‚ÄúYours, sir, would be too much honour for the like of me,‚Äù replied the locksmith, hesitating.

‚ÄúI wish you to remain here a little while, Varden,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝHaredale, without looking towards them. ‚ÄúI have a word or two to say to you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI will not intrude upon your conference another moment,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester with inconceivable politeness. ‚ÄúMay it be satisfactory to you both! God bless you!‚Äù So saying, and bestowing upon the locksmith a most refulgent smile, he left them.

‚ÄúA deplorably constituted creature, that rugged person,‚Äù he said, as he walked along the street; ‚Äúhe is an atrocity that carries its own punishment along with it‚ÅÝ‚Äîa bear that gnaws himself. And here is one of the inestimable advantages of having a perfect command over one‚Äôs inclinations. I have been tempted in these two short interviews, to draw upon that fellow, fifty times. Five men in six would have yielded to the impulse. By suppressing mine, I wound him deeper and more keenly than if I were the best swordsman in all Europe, and he the worst. You are the wise man‚Äôs very last resource,‚Äù he said, tapping the hilt of his weapon; ‚Äúwe can but appeal to you when all else is said and done. To come to you before, and thereby spare our adversaries so much, is a barbarian mode of warfare, quite unworthy of any man with the remotest pretensions to delicacy of feeling, or refinement.‚Äù

He smiled so very pleasantly as he communed with himself after this manner, that a beggar was emboldened to follow for alms, and to dog his footsteps for some distance. He was gratified by the circumstance, feeling it complimentary to his power of feature, and as a reward suffered the man to follow him until he called a chair, when he graciously dismissed him with a fervent blessing.

‚ÄúWhich is as easy as cursing,‚Äù he wisely added, as he took his seat, ‚Äúand more becoming to the face.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîTo Clerkenwell, my good creatures, if you please!‚Äù The chairmen were rendered quite vivacious by having such a courteous burden, and to Clerkenwell they went at a fair round trot.

Alighting at a certain point he had indicated to them upon the road, and paying them something less than they expected from a fare of such gentle speech, he turned into the street in which the locksmith dwelt, and presently stood beneath the shadow of the Golden Key. Mr.¬ÝTappertit, who was hard at work by lamplight, in a corner of the workshop, remained unconscious of his presence until a hand upon his shoulder made him start and turn his head.

‚ÄúIndustry,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, ‚Äúis the soul of business, and the keystone of prosperity. Mr.¬ÝTappertit, I shall expect you to invite me to dinner when you are Lord Mayor of London.‚Äù

‚ÄúSir,‚Äù returned the ‚Äôprentice, laying down his hammer, and rubbing his nose on the back of a very sooty hand, ‚ÄúI scorn the Lord Mayor and everything that belongs to him. We must have another state of society, sir, before you catch me being Lord Mayor. How de do, sir?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe better, Mr.¬ÝTappertit, for looking into your ingenuous face once more. I hope you are well.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am as well, sir,‚Äù said Sim, standing up to get nearer to his ear, and whispering hoarsely, ‚Äúas any man can be under the aggrawations to which I am exposed. My life‚Äôs a burden to me. If it wasn‚Äôt for wengeance, I‚Äôd play at pitch and toss with it on the losing hazard.‚Äù

‚ÄúIs Mrs.¬ÝVarden at home?‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester.

‚ÄúSir,‚Äù returned Sim, eyeing him over with a look of concentrated expression‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúshe is. Did you wish to see her?‚Äù

Mr.¬ÝChester nodded.

‚ÄúThen come this way, sir,‚Äù said Sim, wiping his face upon his apron. ‚ÄúFollow me, sir.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWould you permit me to whisper in your ear, one half a second?‚Äù

‚ÄúBy all means.‚Äù

Mr.¬ÝTappertit raised himself on tiptoe, applied his lips to Mr.¬ÝChester‚Äôs ear, drew back his head without saying anything, looked hard at him, applied them to his ear again, again drew back, and finally whispered‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúThe name is Joseph Willet. Hush! I say no more.‚Äù

Having said that much, he beckoned the visitor with a mysterious aspect to follow him to the parlour-door, where he announced him in the voice of a gentleman-usher. ‚ÄúMr.¬ÝChester.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd not Mr.¬ÝEd‚Äôdard, mind,‚Äù said Sim, looking into the door again, and adding this by way of postscript in his own person; ‚Äúit‚Äôs his father.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut do not let his father,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, advancing hat in hand, as he observed the effect of this last explanatory announcement, ‚Äúdo not let his father be any check or restraint on your domestic occupations, Miss Varden.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh! Now! There! An‚Äôt I always a-saying it!‚Äù exclaimed Miggs, clapping her hands. ‚ÄúIf he an‚Äôt been and took Missis for her own daughter. Well, she do look like it, that she do. Only think of that, mim!‚Äù

‚ÄúIs it possible,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester in his softest tones, ‚Äúthat this is Mrs.¬ÝVarden! I am amazed. That is not your daughter, Mrs.¬ÝVarden? No, no. Your sister.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy daughter, indeed, sir,‚Äù returned Mrs.¬ÝV., blushing with great juvenility.

‚ÄúAh, Mrs.¬ÝVarden!‚Äù cried the visitor. ‚ÄúAh, ma‚Äôam‚ÅÝ‚Äîhumanity is indeed a happy lot, when we can repeat ourselves in others, and still be young as they. You must allow me to salute you‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe custom of the country, my dear madam‚ÅÝ‚Äîyour daughter too.‚Äù

Dolly showed some reluctance to perform this ceremony, but was sharply reproved by Mrs.¬ÝVarden, who insisted on her undergoing it that minute. For pride, she said with great severity, was one of the seven deadly sins, and humility and lowliness of heart were virtues. Wherefore she desired that Dolly would be kissed immediately, on pain of her just displeasure; at the same time giving her to understand that whatever she saw her mother do, she might safely do herself, without being at the trouble of any reasoning or reflection on the subject‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhich, indeed, was offensive and undutiful, and in direct contravention of the church catechism.

Thus admonished, Dolly complied, though by no means willingly; for there was a broad, bold look of admiration in Mr.¬ÝChester‚Äôs face, refined and polished though it sought to be, which distressed her very much. As she stood with downcast eyes, not liking to look up and meet his, he gazed upon her with an approving air, and then turned to her mother.

‚ÄúMy friend Gabriel (whose acquaintance I only made this very evening) should be a happy man, Mrs.¬ÝVarden.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh!‚Äù sighed Mrs.¬ÝV., shaking her head.

‚ÄúAh!‚Äù echoed Miggs.

‚ÄúIs that the case?‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, compassionately. ‚ÄúDear me!‚Äù

‚ÄúMaster has no intentions, sir,‚Äù murmured Miggs as she sidled up to him, ‚Äúbut to be as grateful as his natur will let him, for everythink he owns which it is in his powers to appreciate. But we never, sir‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîsaid Miggs, looking sideways at Mrs.¬ÝVarden, and interlarding her discourse with a sigh‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúwe never know the full value of some wines and fig-trees till we lose ‚Äôem. So much the worse, sir, for them as has the slighting of ‚Äôem on their consciences when they‚Äôre gone to be in full blow elsewhere.‚Äù And Miss Miggs cast up her eyes to signify where that might be.

As Mrs.¬ÝVarden distinctly heard, and was intended to hear, all that Miggs said, and as these words appeared to convey in metaphorical terms a presage or foreboding that she would at some early period droop beneath her trials and take an easy flight towards the stars, she immediately began to languish, and taking a volume of the Manual from a neighbouring table, leant her arm upon it as though she were Hope and that her Anchor. Mr.¬ÝChester perceiving this, and seeing how the volume was lettered on the back, took it gently from her hand, and turned the fluttering leaves.

‚ÄúMy favourite book, dear madam. How often, how very often in his early life‚ÅÝ‚Äîbefore he can remember‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äî(this clause was strictly true) ‚Äúhave I deduced little easy moral lessons from its pages, for my dear son Ned! You know Ned?‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden had that honour, and a fine affable young gentleman he was.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre a mother, Mrs.¬ÝVarden,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, taking a pinch of snuff, ‚Äúand you know what I, as a father, feel, when he is praised. He gives me some uneasiness‚ÅÝ‚Äîmuch uneasiness‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe‚Äôs of a roving nature, ma‚Äôam‚ÅÝ‚Äîfrom flower to flower‚ÅÝ‚Äîfrom sweet to sweet‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut his is the butterfly time of life, and we must not be hard upon such trifling.‚Äù

He glanced at Dolly. She was attending evidently to what he said. Just what he desired!

‚ÄúThe only thing I object to in this little trait of Ned‚Äôs, is,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, ‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚Äîand the mention of his name reminds me, by the way, that I am about to beg the favour of a minute‚Äôs talk with you alone‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe only thing I object to in it, is, that it does partake of insincerity. Now, however I may attempt to disguise the fact from myself in my affection for Ned, still I always revert to this‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat if we are not sincere, we are nothing. Nothing upon earth. Let us be sincere, my dear madam‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚Äîand Protestant,‚Äù murmured Mrs.¬ÝVarden.

‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚Äîand Protestant above all things. Let us be sincere and Protestant, strictly moral, strictly just (though always with a leaning towards mercy), strictly honest, and strictly true, and we gain‚ÅÝ‚Äîit is a slight point, certainly, but still it is something tangible; we throw up a groundwork and foundation, so to speak, of goodness, on which we may afterwards erect some worthy superstructure.‚Äù

Now, to be sure, Mrs.¬ÝVarden thought, here is a perfect character. Here is a meek, righteous, thoroughgoing Christian, who, having mastered all these qualities, so difficult of attainment; who, having dropped a pinch of salt on the tails of all the cardinal virtues, and caught them every one; makes light of their possession, and pants for more morality. For the good woman never doubted (as many good men and women never do), that this slighting kind of profession, this setting so little store by great matters, this seeming to say, ‚ÄúI am not proud, I am what you hear, but I consider myself no better than other people; let us change the subject, pray‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîwas perfectly genuine and true. He so contrived it, and said it in that way that it appeared to have been forced from him, and its effect was marvellous.

Aware of the impression he had made‚ÅÝ‚Äîfew men were quicker than he at such discoveries‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMr.¬ÝChester followed up the blow by propounding certain virtuous maxims, somewhat vague and general in their nature, doubtless, and occasionally partaking of the character of truisms, worn a little out at elbow, but delivered in so charming a voice and with such uncommon serenity and peace of mind, that they answered as well as the best. Nor is this to be wondered at; for as hollow vessels produce a far more musical sound in falling than those which are substantial, so it will oftentimes be found that sentiments which have nothing in them make the loudest ringing in the world, and are the most relished.

Mr.¬ÝChester, with the volume gently extended in one hand, and with the other planted lightly on his breast, talked to them in the most delicious manner possible; and quite enchanted all his hearers, notwithstanding their conflicting interests and thoughts. Even Dolly, who, between his keen regards and her eyeing over by Mr.¬ÝTappertit, was put quite out of countenance, could not help owning within herself that he was the sweetest-spoken gentleman she had ever seen. Even Miss Miggs, who was divided between admiration of Mr.¬ÝChester and a mortal jealousy of her young mistress, had sufficient leisure to be propitiated. Even Mr.¬ÝTappertit, though occupied as we have seen in gazing at his heart‚Äôs delight, could not wholly divert his thoughts from the voice of the other charmer. Mrs.¬ÝVarden, to her own private thinking, had never been so improved in all her life; and when Mr.¬ÝChester, rising and craving permission to speak with her apart, took her by the hand and led her at arm‚Äôs length upstairs to the best sitting-room, she almost deemed him something more than human.

‚ÄúDear madam,‚Äù he said, pressing her hand delicately to his lips; ‚Äúbe seated.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden called up quite a courtly air, and became seated.

‚ÄúYou guess my object?‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, drawing a chair towards her. ‚ÄúYou divine my purpose? I am an affectionate parent, my dear Mrs.¬ÝVarden.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat I am sure you are, sir,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝV.

‚ÄúThank you,‚Äù returned Mr.¬ÝChester, tapping his snuffbox lid. ‚ÄúHeavy moral responsibilities rest with parents, Mrs.¬ÝVarden.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden slightly raised her hands, shook her head, and looked at the ground as though she saw straight through the globe, out at the other end, and into the immensity of space beyond.

‚ÄúI may confide in you,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, ‚Äúwithout reserve. I love my son, ma‚Äôam, dearly; and loving him as I do, I would save him from working certain misery. You know of his attachment to Miss Haredale. You have abetted him in it, and very kind of you it was to do so. I am deeply obliged to you‚ÅÝ‚Äîmost deeply obliged to you‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor your interest in his behalf; but my dear ma‚Äôam, it is a mistaken one, I do assure you.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden stammered that she was sorry‚ÅÝ‚Äî

‚ÄúSorry, my dear ma‚Äôam,‚Äù he interposed. ‚ÄúNever be sorry for what is so very amiable, so very good in intention, so perfectly like yourself. But there are grave and weighty reasons, pressing family considerations, and apart even from these, points of religious difference, which interpose themselves, and render their union impossible; utterly im‚Äëpossible. I should have mentioned these circumstances to your husband; but he has‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou will excuse my saying this so freely‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe has not your quickness of apprehension or depth of moral sense. What an extremely airy house this is, and how beautifully kept! For one like myself‚ÅÝ‚Äîa widower so long‚ÅÝ‚Äîthese tokens of female care and superintendence have inexpressible charms.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden began to think (she scarcely knew why) that the young Mr.¬ÝChester must be in the wrong and the old Mr.¬ÝChester must be in the right.

‚ÄúMy son Ned,‚Äù resumed her tempter with his most winning air, ‚Äúhas had, I am told, your lovely daughter‚Äôs aid, and your openhearted husband‚Äôs.‚Äù

‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMuch more than mine, sir,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝVarden; ‚Äúa great deal more. I have often had my doubts. It‚Äôs a‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúA bad example,‚Äù suggested Mr.¬ÝChester. ‚ÄúIt is. No doubt it is. Your daughter is at that age when to set before her an encouragement for young persons to rebel against their parents on this most important point, is particularly injudicious. You are quite right. I ought to have thought of that myself, but it escaped me, I confess‚ÅÝ‚Äîso far superior are your sex to ours, dear madam, in point of penetration and sagacity.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden looked as wise as if she had really said something to deserve this compliment‚ÅÝ‚Äîfirmly believed she had, in short‚ÅÝ‚Äîand her faith in her own shrewdness increased considerably.

‚ÄúMy dear ma‚Äôam,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, ‚Äúyou embolden me to be plain with you. My son and I are at variance on this point. The young lady and her natural guardian differ upon it, also. And the closing point is, that my son is bound by his duty to me, by his honour, by every solemn tie and obligation, to marry someone else.‚Äù

‚ÄúEngaged to marry another lady!‚Äù quoth Mrs.¬ÝVarden, holding up her hands.

‚ÄúMy dear madam, brought up, educated, and trained, expressly for that purpose. Expressly for that purpose.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMiss Haredale, I am told, is a very charming creature.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am her foster-mother, and should know‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe best young lady in the world,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝVarden.

‚ÄúI have not the smallest doubt of it. I am sure she is. And you, who have stood in that tender relation towards her, are bound to consult her happiness. Now, can I‚ÅÝ‚Äîas I have said to Haredale, who quite agrees‚ÅÝ‚Äîcan I possibly stand by, and suffer her to throw herself away (although she is of a Catholic family), upon a young fellow who, as yet, has no heart at all? It is no imputation upon him to say he has not, because young men who have plunged deeply into the frivolities and conventionalities of society, very seldom have. Their hearts never grow, my dear ma‚Äôam, till after thirty. I don‚Äôt believe, no, I do not believe, that I had any heart myself when I was Ned‚Äôs age.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh sir,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝVarden, ‚ÄúI think you must have had. It‚Äôs impossible that you, who have so much now, can ever have been without any.‚Äù

‚ÄúI hope,‚Äù he answered, shrugging his shoulders meekly, ‚ÄúI have a little; I hope, a very little‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHeaven knows! But to return to Ned; I have no doubt you thought, and therefore interfered benevolently in his behalf, that I objected to Miss Haredale. How very natural! My dear madam, I object to him‚ÅÝ‚Äîto him‚ÅÝ‚Äîemphatically to Ned himself.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden was perfectly aghast at the disclosure.

‚ÄúHe has, if he honourably fulfils this solemn obligation of which I have told you‚ÅÝ‚Äîand he must be honourable, dear Mrs.¬ÝVarden, or he is no son of mine‚ÅÝ‚Äîa fortune within his reach. He is of most expensive, ruinously expensive habits; and if, in a moment of caprice and wilfulness, he were to marry this young lady, and so deprive himself of the means of gratifying the tastes to which he has been so long accustomed, he would‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy dear madam, he would break the gentle creature‚Äôs heart. Mrs.¬ÝVarden, my good lady, my dear soul, I put it to you‚ÅÝ‚Äîis such a sacrifice to be endured? Is the female heart a thing to be trifled with in this way? Ask your own, my dear madam. Ask your own, I beseech you.‚Äù

‚ÄúTruly,‚Äù thought Mrs.¬ÝVarden, ‚Äúthis gentleman is a saint. But,‚Äù she added aloud, and not unnaturally, ‚Äúif you take Miss Emma‚Äôs lover away, sir, what becomes of the poor thing‚Äôs heart then?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe very point,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝChester, not at all abashed, ‚Äúto which I wished to lead you. A marriage with my son, whom I should be compelled to disown, would be followed by years of misery; they would be separated, my dear madam, in a twelvemonth. To break off this attachment, which is more fancied than real, as you and I know very well, will cost the dear girl but a few tears, and she is happy again. Take the case of your own daughter, the young lady downstairs, who is your breathing image‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMrs.¬ÝVarden coughed and simpered‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúthere is a young man (I am sorry to say, a dissolute fellow, of very indifferent character) of whom I have heard Ned speak‚ÅÝ‚ÄîBullet was it‚ÅÝ‚ÄîPullet‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMullet‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúThere is a young man of the name of Joseph Willet, sir,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝVarden, folding her hands loftily.

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs he,‚Äù cried Mr.¬ÝChester. ‚ÄúSuppose this Joseph Willet now, were to aspire to the affections of your charming daughter, and were to engage them.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt would be like his impudence,‚Äù interposed Mrs.¬ÝVarden, bridling, ‚Äúto dare to think of such a thing!‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear madam, that‚Äôs the whole case. I know it would be like his impudence. It is like Ned‚Äôs impudence to do as he has done; but you would not on that account, or because of a few tears from your beautiful daughter, refrain from checking their inclinations in their birth. I meant to have reasoned thus with your husband when I saw him at Mrs.¬ÝRudge‚Äôs this evening‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúMy husband,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝVarden, interposing with emotion, ‚Äúwould be a great deal better at home than going to Mrs.¬ÝRudge‚Äôs so often. I don‚Äôt know what he does there. I don‚Äôt see what occasion he has to busy himself in her affairs at all, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúIf I don‚Äôt appear to express my concurrence in those last sentiments of yours,‚Äù returned Mr.¬ÝChester, ‚Äúquite so strongly as you might desire, it is because his being there, my dear madam, and not proving conversational, led me hither, and procured me the happiness of this interview with one, in whom the whole management, conduct, and prosperity of her family are centred, I perceive.‚Äù

With that he took Mrs.¬ÝVarden‚Äôs hand again, and having pressed it to his lips with the highflown gallantry of the day‚ÅÝ‚Äîa little burlesqued to render it the more striking in the good lady‚Äôs unaccustomed eyes‚ÅÝ‚Äîproceeded in the same strain of mingled sophistry, cajolery, and flattery, to entreat that her utmost influence might be exerted to restrain her husband and daughter from any further promotion of Edward‚Äôs suit to Miss Haredale, and from aiding or abetting either party in any way. Mrs.¬ÝVarden was but a woman, and had her share of vanity, obstinacy, and love of power. She entered into a secret treaty of alliance, offensive and defensive, with her insinuating visitor; and really did believe, as many others would have done who saw and heard him, that in so doing she furthered the ends of truth, justice, and morality, in a very uncommon degree.

Overjoyed by the success of his negotiation, and mightily amused within himself, Mr.¬ÝChester conducted her downstairs in the same state as before; and having repeated the previous ceremony of salutation, which also as before comprehended Dolly, took his leave; first completing the conquest of Miss Miggs‚Äôs heart, by inquiring if ‚Äúthis young lady‚Äù would light him to the door.

‚ÄúOh, mim,‚Äù said Miggs, returning with the candle. ‚ÄúOh gracious me, mim, there‚Äôs a gentleman! Was there ever such an angel to talk as he is‚ÅÝ‚Äîand such a sweet-looking man! So upright and noble, that he seems to despise the very ground he walks on; and yet so mild and condescending, that he seems to say ‚Äòbut I will take notice on it too.‚Äô And to think of his taking you for Miss Dolly, and Miss Dolly for your sister‚ÅÝ‚ÄîOh, my goodness me, if I was master wouldn‚Äôt I be jealous of him!‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝVarden reproved her handmaid for this vain-speaking; but very gently and mildly‚ÅÝ‚Äîquite smilingly indeed‚ÅÝ‚Äîremarking that she was a foolish, giddy, lightheaded girl, whose spirits carried her beyond all bounds, and who didn‚Äôt mean half she said, or she would be quite angry with her.

‚ÄúFor my part,‚Äù said Dolly, in a thoughtful manner, ‚ÄúI half believe Mr.¬ÝChester is something like Miggs in that respect. For all his politeness and pleasant speaking, I am pretty sure he was making game of us, more than once.‚Äù

‚ÄúIf you venture to say such a thing again, and to speak ill of people behind their backs in my presence, miss,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝVarden, ‚ÄúI shall insist upon your taking a candle and going to bed directly. How dare you, Dolly? I‚Äôm astonished at you. The rudeness of your whole behaviour this evening has been disgraceful. Did anybody ever hear,‚Äù cried the enraged matron, bursting into tears, ‚Äúof a daughter telling her own mother she has been made game of!‚Äù

What a very uncertain temper Mrs.¬ÝVarden‚Äôs was!
